answer:
Surah Ash-Shura 42:11 establishes the fundamental rule that there is absolutely no similarity between the Creator and His creation in essence, attributes, or deeds [1]. This ayah anchors the theological framework that believers must accept God's attributes exactly as He has described them, without denying them, applying allegorical meanings, or comparing them to human qualities [2].

Surah Al-Ikhlas 112:1-4 provides a concise, undefiled concept of God’s oneness that eliminates all polytheistic concepts and leaves no room for ascribing human qualities to His Being [3, 4]. It definitively states that God is single and unique, the Eternal Refuge, who does not beget, is not born, and has no equivalent [5].

Several other primary ayat directly support the scriptural framework for studying the attributes of Allah:

*   **Surah Al-A'raf 7:180 and Surah Taha 20:8:** These ayat declare that "to God belong the best names" [6, 7]. They command believers to invoke Him using these names and to distance themselves from those who practice deviation concerning His names [6].
*   **Surah Al-Hashr 59:22-24:** This passage provides a detailed exposition of His divine attributes, affirming that He is the Sovereign, the Pure, the Perfection, the Creator, the Inventor, and the Fashioner [8, 9].
*   **Surah Al-Baqarah 2:255 (Ayat al-Kursi):** This ayah highlights His absolute perfection and boundless knowledge, affirming that He is the Ever-Living and Sustainer of existence who never experiences human limitations such as sleep or drowsiness [10].

10. And in anything over which you disag-

their sight. Every time it lights [the way] for them, they walk therein; but when darkness comes over them, they stand [still]. And if God had willed, He could have taken away their hearing and their sight. Indeed, God is over all things competent.
10. And in anything over which you disag-

their sight. Every time it lights [the way] for them, they walk therein; but when darkness comes over them, they stand [still]. And if God had willed, He could have taken away their hearing and their sight. Indeed, God is over all things competent.
10. And in anything over which you disag-

their sight. Every time it lights [the way] for them, they walk therein; but when darkness comes over them, they stand [still]. And if God had willed, He could have taken away their hearing and their sight. Indeed, God is over all things competent.

This Sūrah teaches Tawhid, pure and undefiled. Therefore, the Prophet regarded it as equal to one-third of the Qur’ān.  A tradition on the authority of Ā’isha has been related in Bukhari, Muslim and other collections of the ha-dith, saying that the Prophet sent a man as leader of an expedition. During the journey he concluded his reci-tation of the Qur’ān in every Prayer with Qul Huwa-Allahu ahad. On their return him companions men-tioned this before the Prophet. He said: “Ask him why he did so.” When the man was asked, he replied: “In this Sūrah the attributes of the Merciful God have been stated; therefore, I love to recite it again and again.” When the Prophet heard this reply, he said to the people: “Inform him that God holds him in great love and esteem.”  A similar incident has been related in Bukhari, on the authority of Anas. He says: “A man from among the Ansār led the Prayers in the Quba Mosque.

their sight. Every time it lights [the way] for them, they walk therein; but when darkness comes over them, they stand [still]. And if God had willed, He could have taken away their hearing and their sight. Indeed, God is over all things competent.
